stories.original:
**Keel pivots from neobank to BaaS platform, enters crowded UK infrastructure market**

*A UK fintech has abandoned its consumer banking ambitions in favour of selling payments and open banking infrastructure to other companies, joining a competitive and consolidating banking-as-a-service segment.*

Keel has emerged from stealth as a banking-as-a-service provider, offering ledger management, open banking capabilities and access to domestic and international payment rails such as Faster Payments, Bacs, CHAPS, SEPA, SWIFT, ACH and Fedwire. The company previously operated as a neobank before deciding the infrastructure layer offered a more viable business.

The pivot is worth noting less for what Keel itself offers — its capabilities are broadly comparable to established players such as ClearBank, Griffin and Modulr — than for what it says about the economics of UK consumer banking versus infrastructure provision. Several BaaS providers have faced regulatory and commercial pressure in recent years, and the segment has seen both exits and consolidation. Keel enters at a moment when buyers are more discerning about counterparty resilience and regulatory standing. Whether there is room for another entrant will depend on whether Keel can differentiate on reliability and compliance rather than feature lists alone.

*Editorial note: The neobank-to-infrastructure pivot is a meaningful pattern in UK fintech, and the story offers a window into the competitive dynamics of BaaS at a time of ongoing consolidation.*

*Sources: [UK BaaS platform Keel emerges from stealth](https://www.finextra.com/newsarticle/47727/uk-baas-platform-keel-emerges-from-stealth) — Finextra | [Keel unveils fintech infrastructure business after pivot from neobank](https://tech.eu/2026/05/11/keel-unveils-fintech-infrastructure-business-after-pivot-from-neobank/) — Tech.eu*

stories.original:
**European banks weigh stablecoins for payments and treasury management**

*A growing number of European banks are exploring euro-denominated stablecoins as complements to existing payment rails, positioning them alongside instant payments and bank deposits rather than as alternatives to them.*

IBS Intelligence reports that European banks are actively evaluating stablecoins for use in payments and treasury operations, framing them not as disruptors to conventional infrastructure but as additional instruments within the regulated financial system. The emphasis is on euro-denominated, bank-issued or bank-partnered tokens that sit within existing compliance frameworks.

The timing is significant. With MiCA now providing a regulatory baseline for stablecoin issuance in the EU, banks have a clearer path to experimentation than they did even a year ago. The framing matters: institutions are positioning stablecoins alongside instant payments and deposits, suggesting they see programmability and 24/7 settlement as the value proposition, not disintermediation. For payments professionals, the question is whether stablecoins will find durable use cases that instant payments rails such as SEPA Instant cannot serve — or whether they will remain a niche treasury tool.

*Editorial note: European bank engagement with stablecoins is moving from theoretical to operational, and the interplay with instant payments infrastructure is directly relevant to the audience.*

*Sources: [European banks explore stablecoins for payments and treasury](https://ibsintelligence.com/ibsi-news/european-banks-explore-stablecoins-for-payments-and-treasury/) — IBS Intelligence*

stories.original:
**Visa selects seven fintechs from Greece, Cyprus and Malta for European innovation programme**

*Visa's 2026 European innovation cohort focuses on open finance and data, targeting southeast European markets that are often underrepresented in pan-European fintech programmes.*

Visa has chosen seven startups from Cyprus, Greece and Malta for the latest edition of its Visa Innovation Program Europe. The cohort is organised around themes such as open finance and data, with selected companies expected to work on scalable solutions relevant to the broader European market.

The geographic focus is the notable element. Southeast European markets have smaller fintech ecosystems but are subject to the same EU regulatory trajectory — FiDA, PSD3, SEPA Instant — as larger member states. Programmes like this serve a dual purpose: they give Visa early access to local innovators, and they help smaller-market fintechs build the partnerships needed to scale beyond their home jurisdictions. The selection criteria emphasise practical scalability rather than early-stage novelty, which suggests Visa is looking for companies closer to commercial readiness.

*Editorial note: Visa's cohort selection signals continued institutional interest in open finance across less-covered European markets, relevant to readers tracking EU-wide adoption patterns.*

*Sources: [Visa selects seven startups from Cyprus, Greece, and Malta for innovation hub](https://cyprus-mail.com/2026/05/11/visa-selects-seven-startups-from-cyprus-greece-and-malta-for-innovation-hub) — Cyprus Mail | [Visa Innovation Program Europe: The seven fintechs selected for 2026 edition](https://www.cbn.com.cy/article/128956/visa-innovation-program-europe-the-seven-fintechs-selected-for-2026-edition-across-greece-cyprus-and-malta) — CBN*

stories.original:
**Lloyds highlights open banking data as route to credit inclusion**

*Lloyds Banking Group's latest financial empowerment report identifies open banking transaction data, alongside rent and utility payments, as a means to extend credit access to underserved consumers.*

In a report published on its corporate site, Lloyds argues that incorporating alternative data sources — such as rent payments, utility bills and open banking transaction data — into credit scoring could unlock mainstream credit for consumers currently excluded by traditional models. The report frames this as part of a broader financial wellbeing strategy.

The substance is not new: the use of open banking data in creditworthiness assessment has been discussed since PSD2's early days, and several fintechs already offer it. What is notable is a UK high-street bank stating it publicly and tying it to a corporate strategy document. For open banking advocates, this is useful evidence that incumbents see commercial and reputational value in data-driven inclusion, not just regulatory compliance. Whether Lloyds is actively building these capabilities or simply acknowledging the direction of travel is not clear from the report alone.

*Editorial note: A major UK incumbent publicly endorsing open banking data for credit scoring is a meaningful signal of mainstream adoption, even if the underlying idea is well-established.*

*Sources: [Lives empowered, a nation empowered](https://www.lloydsbankinggroup.com/who-we-are/our-strategy/financial-wellbeing/financial-empowerment-report.html) — Lloyds Banking Group*

stories.original:
**SAMA grants its 32nd payment services licence as Saudi open banking expands**

*Saudi Arabia's central bank has licensed Tatbiq Darahem Company for Technology Almaliya to provide open banking services, bringing the total number of licensed payment services providers in the kingdom to 32.*

SAMA's latest licence continues the rapid expansion of Saudi Arabia's regulated payments and open banking ecosystem. Tatbiq Darahem joins a growing roster of authorised firms as the kingdom builds out its open finance infrastructure in parallel with its broader economic diversification agenda.

For European observers, the Saudi trajectory offers a useful comparator. The kingdom has moved quickly from framework to licensing at a pace that contrasts with the EU's more protracted legislative process around FiDA. With 32 licensed providers now active, Saudi Arabia is generating competitive density that some European markets have struggled to achieve. The practical question is whether licensing volume translates into consumer and business adoption, or whether the market fragments before meaningful use cases take hold.

*Editorial note: Saudi Arabia's licensing pace provides a relevant benchmark for European readers tracking open banking adoption across competing regulatory regimes.*

*Sources: [SAMA licenses Tatbiq Darahem Company for Technology Almaliya to provide open banking services](https://www.zawya.com/en/business/fintech/sauds-sama-licenses-tatbiq-darahem-company-for-technology-almaliya-to-provide-open-banking-services-lm5064zn) — Zawya*

---
